text: The surgeon identifies the ureter and retracts it medially out of the operating field during lateral pelvic lymph node dissection. The ureter is dissected along with the ureterohypogastric fascia, establishing the medial margin of the dissection while keeping the fascia intact to protect the hypogastric nerve plexus.

The surgeon focuses on the external iliac vessels, dissecting lymphofatty tissues from the surface of the external iliac artery and vein. The dissection progresses deeper laterally until the obturator internus muscles are visible, establishing the lateral margin of the pelvic lymph node dissection.

During deeper dissection, the obturator nerve is visible between the bifurcation of the external and internal iliac veins. The nerve is preserved unless it is grossly involved by metastatic lymph nodes.
The surgeon dissects and ligates the root of the obturator artery, which bifurcates from the internal iliac artery. The proximal and distal ends of the obturator vein are also ligated.

The surgeon dissects lymphofatty tissue along the obturator nerve, progressing towards the obturator foramen. The distal end of the obturator artery is ligated, followed by the obturator vein, ensuring complete clearance of lymphatic tissue within the obturator space.
The surgeon continues distal dissection, revealing the deep circumflex iliac vein, which marks the most caudal part of the dissection anteriorly.

The surgeon performs dissection along the vesico-hypogastric plane, exposing the internal iliac artery. The branches of the anterior internal iliac artery are carefully dissected. The umbilical artery is dissected and ligated at its root, while the superior vesicle artery is preserved. The visual field shows the potential for sacrificing all branches of the internal iliac artery if required.

The surgeon dissects lymphatic tissue located between the internal iliac vein and ureterohypogastric fascia. The inferior vesicle artery is dissected and ligated at its root. Dissection continues between the bladder and lymphatic tissue, with distal vesicle artery and veins ligated at the bladder side using advanced energy devices or hemlock clips.
The surgeon dissects the most caudal part of the lymphatic tissue beyond the vesicle vessels, within a space bounded by the lateral pelvic sidewall, levator ani muscles, and medial pelvic plexuses.

The surgeon places the resected specimen into a bag for removal, having achieved complete clearance of the lateral pelvic lymph nodes. The hypogastric nerve plexus is preserved, with intersecting branches and veins of the internal iliac artery ligated.
